Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I couldn't always make it to campus or have consistent internet access at home. I'd usually set aside late evenings and weekends for studying since that's when things were quieter and I could focus. I relied heavily on listening to recorded lectures and audio materials because that's how information sticks best for me. I'd often have to plan around when I could access resources, so I'd download what I could and work with that offline.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd have a good rhythm going, but then something would come up—family responsibilities or just feeling overwhelmed—and everything would shift. Deadlines definitely pushed me to study more intensely in bursts. I tried to be consistent, but with everything going on, it was more about surviving each week than following a perfect schedule.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or assignments posted that I needed to know about. Sometimes I'd log in specifically to grab lecture recordings or materials I could download for later. When deadlines were approaching, I'd be on there more frequently to make sure I wasn't missing anything important. Honestly, I didn't browse around much—I went in with a purpose and got out.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt like something I naturally gravitated toward as part of my daily routine. I used it when I needed to, but it always felt more like a tool I had to access rather than something integrated into my habits. My routine was more about the actual studying I did offline with my notes and recordings.
Q5: The lecture recordings were everything to me—being an auditory learner, I could listen to them multiple times until concepts clicked. The discussion forums were also helpful when other students explained things in different ways. I appreciated when instructors posted clear audio explanations alongside the written materials. Those resources felt like they were actually teaching me, not just giving me information to figure out alone.
Q6: I often skipped the video content that was really long because I didn't always have the time or bandwidth to watch them properly. Some of the supplementary readings felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend time on when I was already stretched thin. I focused on what was going to be assessed and what I absolutely needed to understand the core material. Everything else felt like a luxury I couldn't always access.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to what I'd be tested on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. If I could listen to it and learn while doing other things, even better. I also valued activities where I felt like I was making progress, not just going through motions. With limited resources and time, I had to be strategic about what deserved my energy.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like something I'd get to 'if I had time,' which usually meant I didn't get to them. Required activities had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them no matter how anxious or tired I felt. With optional stuff, there was always this guilt because I knew it could help, but I had to prioritize survival over enrichment. It's frustrating because I wanted to do more, but I just couldn't stretch myself that thin.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone when I didn't have internet. I took detailed handwritten notes that I'd review over and over. I also borrowed textbooks from friends and studied from those since I couldn't afford my own copies. The LMS was just the starting point—the actual learning happened away from it.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be up late with my notes and downloaded audio, going through material repeatedly until I understood it. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also have study sessions with a couple of classmates where we'd talk through concepts verbally—that's how I learn best. The LMS only saw me grab the files; it didn't see the hours I spent with them afterward.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture recording once and then listen to it maybe five or six times over the following days. Same with PDF notes—I'd print them out when I could or save them to my phone. One download could turn into a week's worth of study material for me. The system probably thought I barely engaged with something when really I was wearing it out from repeated use.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety got so bad that I couldn't focus on anything academic. When I was feeling really sad or overwhelmed, even opening the LMS felt like too much. My attendance dropped during those times because getting to campus felt impossible some days. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of that, but there were still rough patches where I had to just hold on and do the minimum.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from juggling everything—family stuff, trying to work a bit to help out at home, the emotional weight of it all—the LMS was the first thing to suffer. I'd still study, but in ways that required less from me, like listening to recordings while resting. Confusion with course material also made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face how behind I felt. It was easier to work with what I already had downloaded.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything really sinking in. My mind would be somewhere else—worrying about things at home or just feeling too anxious to concentrate. I'd have the page open, maybe scroll through, but I wasn't actually learning anything. Those moments felt like going through the motions just to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they required me to formulate thoughts and put myself out there, which was hard when I was already feeling low. Some readings got pushed back because they felt less urgent than assignments with hard deadlines. I knew they were important, but my brain was in survival mode—dealing with what was immediately due and letting the rest wait. It wasn't laziness; it was just trying to manage with limited capacity.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd work backward from due dates to figure out what I absolutely had to do and when. My LMS activity probably spiked right before things were due because that's when I'd be uploading assignments and double-checking requirements. Without deadlines, I honestly might have drifted because the structure helped me push through even when I didn't feel like it.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it genuinely lifted my spirits and made me want to engage more with the next assignment. It reminded me that my efforts were paying off despite everything. Negative or critical feedback was harder—it sometimes made me want to avoid that area for a while until I could emotionally handle addressing it. The feedback that explained how to improve was most useful because it gave me something concrete to work toward.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways because I knew what was expected and when. But it also assumed a level of consistent access and presence that I couldn't always maintain with my attendance issues. When things were clearly laid out with deadlines, I could plan around my limitations. The parts that required regular in-person participation were harder for me to keep up with.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier for someone in my situation. What made it harder was when there were activities that required being online at specific times or participating synchronously—I couldn't always make those work. Also, when instructions weren't clear, I'd waste precious time trying to figure out what was actually expected. Clarity and flexibility were what I needed most.
Q20: I think sometimes students are searching for understanding but not finding it, so they keep clicking around without producing work. Or maybe they're dealing with stuff that makes it hard to actually complete things even though they're trying to engage. It could also be that they're anxious about submitting imperfect work, so they prepare endlessly without turning anything in. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just struggling visibly.
Q21: That's basically my story. My LMS numbers probably look unimpressive, but I worked hard with what I had offline. I listened to lectures repeatedly, I studied my notes intensely, I talked through concepts with peers. The grade I earned came from genuine understanding, not from clicking around online. Sometimes the students who look less active are just learning in ways the system can't track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they're accessing, whether they're meeting deadlines. That's useful information. But they miss the depth and quality of engagement entirely. They can't see me listening to a recording for the tenth time or finally understanding a concept while walking home. They measure presence, not learning. And they definitely can't capture what's going on emotionally that affects everything.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious about something and want to figure it out, even when it's hard. For me, it's those moments when I'm actively listening and connecting ideas, whether I'm online or not. True engagement is mental and emotional investment in learning—it's not something you can measure by clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I pushed through despite feeling sad and anxious most of the time. It wouldn't capture the resilience it took to keep going with limited resources and low attendance. It can't see the hours of offline study, the peer conversations, the CBT sessions that helped me stay afloat. My engagement was real and deep—it just happened mostly in places the data couldn't reach.
